components.name: Domain Layer
components.responsibility: Define immutable BuildRun value object with validation and computed properties (duration_seconds, is_success). Enforces invariants: non-empty id, end_time >= start_time.

components.name: Persistence Layer
components.responsibility: JSONL-backed repository for BuildRun persistence. Implements save() for append-only writes and get_last_n() for reading recent runs with corruption handling.

components.name: Service Layer
components.responsibility: Business logic for computing aggregate statistics (average duration, success rate) over BuildRun collections retrieved from repository.

components.name: Module Initialization
components.responsibility: Export public API of build_metrics package: BuildRun, BuildMetricsRepository, MetricsService.

components.name: CLI Integration
components.responsibility: Add record and stats subcommands to CLI. Wire argparse to build_metrics components with error handling.

components.name: Domain Tests
components.responsibility: Unit tests for BuildRun: instantiation, validation, duration computation, serialization roundtrip.

components.name: Persistence Tests
components.responsibility: Unit tests for BuildMetricsRepository: save/read operations, corruption handling, empty file scenarios.

components.name: Service Tests
components.responsibility: Unit tests for MetricsService: statistics computation with mock repository, edge cases (empty dataset, all success/failure).

components.name: CLI Integration Tests
components.responsibility: End-to-end tests for record/stats commands with temporary storage, verifying data persistence and JSON output.

decisions.decision: Use JSONL file format instead of SQLite database
decisions.rationale: Zero external dependencies, human-readable format, append-only simplicity. Acceptable performance for ~10k runs (PRD scope). SQLite adds schema migration complexity without validated benefit. JSONL format is stable for future migration to indexed backend if needed.

decisions.decision: Immutable domain model with frozen dataclass
decisions.rationale: BuildRun represents historical fact that never changes. frozen=True prevents mutation bugs and provides thread-safety. Value semantics align with domain: build runs are immutable observations, not entities to be modified.

decisions.decision: ISO8601 string format for timestamps in JSONL
decisions.rationale: Human-readable, universally supported by datetime.fromisoformat() in Python 3.10+, no timezone ambiguity (all UTC). 30% larger file size than epoch integers is acceptable for human-debuggability. String parsing overhead negligible for <10k runs.

decisions.decision: Separate service layer for business logic
decisions.rationale: Single responsibility: repository handles I/O, service handles computation. Enables unit testing with mock repository (no file I/O in tests). Future stats extensions (median, p95) don't require persistence layer changes.

decisions.decision: Fail-fast error handling with exception propagation
decisions.rationale: Invalid data (end_time < start_time) must never persist. Malformed JSONL lines logged to stderr but don't crash (partial availability preferred). CLI catches exceptions and shows user-friendly error messages.

decisions.decision: No file locking for concurrent writes in v1
decisions.rationale: PRD explicitly lists concurrency as out-of-scope. File locking adds platform-specific complexity (fcntl/msvcrt). Concurrent writes are rare for human-driven CLI tool. Documented limitation acceptable for v1, easily added via fcntl or SQLite migration in v2.

decisions.decision: Linear scan for get_last_n() instead of indexed reads
decisions.rationale: JSONL is append-only sequential format. Linear scan is O(N) but acceptable for ~10k runs (~1ms per 100 lines). Alternative (tail command) adds subprocess dependency and platform variance. Future optimization path: add index file or migrate to SQLite.

decisions.decision: Repository constructor accepts optional storage_path parameter
decisions.rationale: Dependency injection enables test isolation without environment variables or global state. Tests override with tmp_path fixture. Production code uses default ~/.buildstats/metrics.jsonl. Clean separation between production and test configuration.

architecture.file_changes_overview: Create 9 new files: 4 implementation modules (domain.py, persistence.py, service.py, __init__.py in build_metrics package), 5 test modules (test_domain.py, test_persistence.py, test_service.py, test_cli_integration.py, __init__.py in tests/build_metrics). Modify 1 existing file: cli.py (~70 lines added for record/stats subcommands and handler functions). Total new code: ~900 lines. All modules use stdlib only (no external dependencies).
interfaces: BuildRun dataclass: frozen=True, fields (id: str, start_time: datetime, end_time: datetime, exit_status: int), __post_init__() validation, duration_seconds property, is_success() method
BuildMetricsRepository.__init__(storage_path: Optional[Path] = None): Initialize with storage path, defaults to ~/.buildstats/metrics.jsonl
BuildMetricsRepository.save(build_run: BuildRun) -> None: Append build_run to JSONL file, create directory if needed, raise OSError on failure
BuildMetricsRepository.get_last_n(n: int) -> list[BuildRun]: Return last N runs in reverse chronological order, skip corrupted lines with stderr warning, return empty list if file missing or n <= 0
MetricsService.__init__(repository: BuildMetricsRepository): Initialize with repository dependency
MetricsService.compute_stats(last_n: int) -> StatsResult: Compute avg_duration_seconds and success_rate over last N runs, return zeros if empty dataset
StatsResult TypedDict: keys avg_duration_seconds (float) and success_rate (float)
_record_command(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int: Parse ISO8601 timestamps, create BuildRun, save to repository, return 0 on success or 1 on error with stderr message
_stats_command(args: argparse.Namespace) -> int: Compute stats via service, print JSON to stdout, always return 0
JSONL format: {"id": str, "start_time": ISO8601, "end_time": ISO8601, "exit_status": int} one record per line

invariants: exit_status is integer (0 = success per Unix convention)
aggregates.name: BuildRun
aggregates.responsibility: Immutable record of a single build execution with identity (id), temporal data (start_time, end_time), and outcome (exit_status).

domain_services.name: MetricsService
domain_services.responsibility: Compute aggregate statistics over collections of BuildRun objects: average duration (mean of duration_seconds) and success rate (proportion with exit_status == 0).

bounded_contexts.name: Build-Metrics
bounded_contexts.purpose: Persist build run telemetry (id, timing, exit status) to durable storage and provide statistical aggregations (average duration, success rate) over recent builds.

guardrails.enforcement: domain.py, persistence.py, service.py are distinct files. Tests are in separate test_*.py files. No file is modified by multiple agents.
guardrails.rule: Each component must live in a separate file to enable parallel agent execution

guardrails.enforcement: Type signatures in components section are copy-paste ready. Implementation must not deviate from specified signatures.
guardrails.rule: All type signatures must match canonical interfaces defined in architecture

guardrails.enforcement: domain.py imports only dataclasses and datetime from stdlib. No cross-module imports.
guardrails.rule: Domain layer must have zero dependencies beyond stdlib

guardrails.enforcement: CLI catches ValueError for user input errors, OSError for system errors. Service layer never catches exceptions (pure business logic).
guardrails.rule: Error handling must distinguish user errors (exit 1) from system errors (propagate)

guardrails.enforcement: BuildMetricsRepository.__init__ accepts optional storage_path. Tests use tmp_path fixture, production uses default ~/.buildstats/metrics.jsonl.
guardrails.rule: Test isolation via dependency injection (storage_path parameter)

module_contracts.notes: Pure domain model with no external dependencies. Self-contained module enabling parallel implementation.
JSONL serialization format: {id, start_time (ISO8601), end_time (ISO8601), exit_status}. Handles corrupted lines gracefully with stderr warnings.
Business logic layer. Returns zeros for empty datasets (0.0 avg_duration, 0.0 success_rate).

consumes: BuildRun, BuildMetricsRepository, MetricsService from build_metrics package
module_contracts.notes: Wires argparse to domain/persistence/service layers. Error handling: catch ValueError/OSError in record, print to stderr, exit 1.
provides: record subcommand for persisting build runs

observability.detail: BuildMetricsRepository.get_last_n() logs to stderr when skipping malformed JSON lines. Format: 'Warning: Skipping malformed line <N> in <path>: <error>'
observability.name: Corrupted JSONL line warnings

observability.detail: CLI prints 'Error: <message>' to stderr for ValueError (invalid timestamps, temporal ordering violations). Exit code 1.
observability.name: User input validation errors

observability.detail: CLI prints 'Error: <message>' to stderr for OSError (disk full, permission denied). Exit code 1.
observability.name: System I/O errors

vertical_slice.description: End-to-end record-and-query flow: (1) User invokes 'buildstats record' with --id, --start-time, --end-time, --exit-status. (2) CLI parses ISO8601 timestamps, creates BuildRun with validation. (3) Repository serializes to JSONL and appends to ~/.buildstats/metrics.jsonl. (4) User invokes 'buildstats stats --last-n 5'. (5) Service retrieves last 5 runs from repository. (6) Service computes avg_duration_seconds and success_rate. (7) CLI prints JSON to stdout. This slice validates all layers: domain validation, persistence I/O, service aggregation, CLI integration.

architecture.summary: Build-Metrics bounded context implementation with domain layer (BuildRun value object), persistence layer (JSONL repository), service layer (statistics computation), and CLI integration (record/stats subcommands). Zero external dependencies, stdlib-only implementation optimized for parallel agent execution.

acceptance_criteria: Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py --version', when executed, then it exits with code 0
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py --version', when executed, then it outputs exactly one line matching regex '^buildstats \d+\.\d+\.\d+$'
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py --version', when executed, then the version printed matches buildstats.__version__
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py --version', when executed, then exactly 1 line is output
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py --version', when executed without a subcommand, then it works and exits 0
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py -v', when executed, then it produces identical output to --version
Given 'python src/buildstats/cli.py -v', when executed, then it exits with code 0
issues.description: Implements --version/-v flag support in the buildstats CLI that prints 'buildstats <version>' and exits with code 0. The flag must work as a top-level argument before any subcommand, using argparse's version action to read from buildstats.__version__.

guidance.review_focus: Verify argparse version action is configured correctly with both --version and -v flags. Confirm version string format is exactly 'buildstats <version>' with no extra output. Ensure flag works at top-level (before subcommands).
guidance.risk_rationale: Trivial implementation using argparse's built-in version action. Low risk - single-file change with clear behavioral contract. No deeper QA needed.
guidance.testing_guidance: Unit tests in tests/test_cli_version.py using pytest. Test --version and -v flags for: exit code 0, single-line output, correct format 'buildstats X.Y.Z', version matches __version__, works without subcommand. Use subprocess.run or invoke cli.main() directly with SystemExit capture.

issues.name: cli-version-flag
provides: CLI --version flag that prints package version and exits
issues.testing_strategy: Create tests/test_cli_version.py using pytest. Work Red→Green→Refactor. 🔴 Write test_version_flag_exits_zero first: invoke cli.main(['--version']) and assert it raises SystemExit(0) - watch it fail because --version doesn't exist yet. 🔴 Write test_version_output_format: capture stdout, assert output matches 'buildstats \d+\.\d+\.\d+$' pattern. 🔴 Write test_version_matches_package: verify printed version equals buildstats.__version__. 🔴 Write test_short_flag_v: verify -v produces identical behavior. 🔴 Write test_version_single_line: verify exactly one line of output. 🔴 Write test_version_without_subcommand: verify --version works standalone. 🟢 Implement by adding parser.add_argument('--version', '-v', action='version', version='buildstats %(prog)s') to cli.py - this minimal argparse feature makes all tests pass. 🔵 Refactor: ensure version string interpolation is correct and format string uses buildstats.__version__ properly. Covers AC1-AC7.
issues.title: Add --version flag to buildstats CLI

verification.command: test "$(python src/buildstats/cli.py --version 2>&1 | cut -d' ' -f2)" = "$(python -c 'import sys; sys.path.insert(0, "src"); from buildstats import __version__; print(__version__)')"

bus.description: Synchronous in-process event bus using Python dictionary-based pub/sub. Events are dispatched via EventBus.publish(event) and consumed via EventBus.subscribe(event_type, handler). No external queue or broker required.
bus.migration_notes: For v1, events are in-memory only (no persistence). Future versions may introduce async event log (e.g., append-only event store in JSONL) or external queue (Kafka, Redis Streams) if cross-process communication is required. Migration path: wrap in_process bus with adapter pattern to support multiple transports.

extraction_strategy.description: After vertical slice passes integration tests (buildstats record → JSONL persistence → event emission), extract Build-Metrics bounded context into standalone package 'buildstats-metrics' with public API: BuildRun, BuildMetricsRepository, MetricsService. Extraction steps: (1) Copy build_metrics/ to new repo, (2) Add pyproject.toml with dependencies: Python 3.10+, (3) Publish to PyPI, (4) Update buildstats CLI to import from 'buildstats-metrics' package, (5) Keep event schemas backward-compatible (no breaking changes to BuildRunRecorded v1.0.0). Gating criteria: All tests in tests/build_metrics/ pass, CLI integration tests pass, vertical slice validated end-to-end.

guardrails.enforcement: Code review checklist: BuildMetricsRepository methods must only perform CRUD operations (save, get_last_n). Any aggregation logic must live in MetricsService.

module_contracts.notes: Root package exports version metadata; consumed by CLI for --version flag

Persistence layer depends only on domain; emits BuildRunRecorded events on save()
Service layer depends on persistence; emits MetricsComputed events on compute_stats()

observability.detail: Log every CLI invocation to ~/.buildstats/cli.log: timestamp, command, args, exit_code, duration_ms. Format: {"timestamp": "...", "command": "record", "args": {...}, "exit_code": 0, "duration_ms": 15}

read_models.name: AggregateStatistics
read_models.purpose: Materialized view of aggregate build metrics (avg duration, success rate) over sliding windows (last N runs). Updated incrementally when BuildRunRecorded events arrive.

read_models.name: VersionCache
read_models.purpose: In-memory cache of application version string to avoid repeated file I/O when --version is invoked. Invalidated on application restart.

vertical_slice.description: End-to-end slice: User invokes 'buildstats record --id build-001 --start-time 2026-06-26T10:00:00 --end-time 2026-06-26T10:05:00 --exit-status 0' → CLI parses args → Creates BuildRun domain object → Repository saves to JSONL → Emits BuildRunRecorded event → EventBus logs event → Returns exit code 0. Tests validate: (1) JSONL file contains exactly one line, (2) Deserialized BuildRun matches input, (3) Event log contains BuildRunRecorded with correct payload.

acceptance_criteria: python src/buildstats/cli.py --version exits with code 0 (verify: python src/buildstats/cli.py --version; test $? -eq 0)

python src/buildstats/cli.py --version outputs exactly one line matching regex '^buildstats \d+\.\d+\.\d+$' (verify: python src/buildstats/cli.py --version | grep -E '^buildstats [0-9]+\.[0-9]+\.[0-9]+$' | wc -l equals 1)

Version printed matches buildstats.__version__ (verify: test "$(python src/buildstats/cli.py --version | cut -d' ' -f2)" = "$(python -c 'import sys; sys.path.insert(0, "src"); from buildstats import __version__; print(__version__)')")
python src/buildstats/cli.py --version outputs exactly 1 line (verify: test $(python src/buildstats/cli.py --version | wc -l) -eq 1)
python src/buildstats/cli.py --version works without subcommand (verify: python src/buildstats/cli.py --version exits 0 and produces output)
python src/buildstats/cli.py -v produces identical output to --version (verify: test "$(python src/buildstats/cli.py -v)" = "$(python src/buildstats/cli.py --version)")
python src/buildstats/cli.py -v exits with code 0 (verify: python src/buildstats/cli.py -v; test $? -eq 0)
assumptions: __version__ in src/buildstats/__init__.py is the authoritative version source
Version string format in __init__.py is always valid semantic versioning (X.Y.Z)
Version output goes to stdout, not stderr (Unix convention)
argparse action='version' is the idiomatic approach for implementing --version
Relative import 'from buildstats import __version__' works within the package structure
Exiting after printing version without executing subcommand logic is correct behavior
must_have: Add --version flag to argparse.ArgumentParser in src/buildstats/cli.py using action='version'
Add -v as short form alias for --version flag
Import __version__ from buildstats module at top of src/buildstats/cli.py
Set version string to 'buildstats {__version__}' format using f-string or string formatting
Ensure version flag is added to main parser (not subparser) so it works without subcommand
Version flag automatically exits with code 0 (standard argparse action='version' behavior)
out_of_scope: Adding version information to subcommand help text
Adding version metadata to buildstats run output
Modifying existing help text beyond adding version flag
Changing command structure or subcommand behavior
risks: Import path issues if package not installed or PYTHONPATH not set - mitigated by testing from repo root with correct paths; accepted as standard Python behavior
Version drift between __init__.py and pyproject.toml - not addressed in this feature; accepted as broader release engineering concern outside scope
prd.validated_description: Add a --version flag to the buildstats CLI that prints the package version string and exits with code 0. The version string format will be 'buildstats <version>' where <version> is read from buildstats.__version__. The flag must work as a top-level argument (before any subcommand) and must not require a subcommand to be specified.

rationale: This is a single-issue decomposition for a trivial feature. The --version flag is a self-contained CLI enhancement that requires modifying only cli.py to add argparse's built-in version action and creating a focused test file. The implementation is straightforward (argparse handles version printing and exit behavior), making this an atomic unit of work completable in one session. No dependencies exist, and the change doesn't touch any other modules. The testing strategy follows TDD: write failing tests for all 7 acceptance criteria first, then implement the minimal argparse configuration to make them pass, then refactor if needed. Verification commands directly map to each acceptance criterion for deterministic validation.

review.feedback:
Architecture is comprehensive and ready for implementation. All acceptance criteria have clear implementation paths, interfaces are precisely specified, and internal consistency is maintained throughout.

**Strengths:**

1. **Requirements traceability**: Every acceptance criterion maps to specific components with clear implementation paths. The validation in BuildRun.__post_init__ prevents invalid data (AC4), the StatsResult TypedDict guarantees correct JSON output (AC6), and edge cases like empty datasets are explicitly handled (AC9).

2. **Interface precision**: All type signatures are canonical and complete (lines 690-736). Error handling strategies are explicit with documented exception types and recovery mechanisms. Edge cases are covered: n <= 0 returns empty list, missing files return empty list, malformed JSONL lines are skipped with warnings.

3. **Internal consistency**: The StatsResult TypedDict matches JSON output format, JSONL serialization matches deserialization, and the dependency DAG is valid (domain → persistence → service → CLI). Data flow examples align with method signatures.

4. **Complexity calibration**: Appropriately complex for the problem. JSONL format is simple yet sufficient for < 10k runs. Three-layer architecture is justified by testability requirements (AC11). No over-engineering (no unnecessary abstractions) and no under-engineering (validation and error handling present).

5. **Scope alignment**: Implements all must-haves without scope creep. No nice-to-have features added. All PRD assumptions honored (storage location, JSONL format, exit status convention, empty set behavior, ordering, UTC). All risk mitigations present (large file warning at 10k lines, malformed data handling, clock skew validation, disk full propagation).

**Implementation notes for autonomous agents:**

- Follow the execution order in the dependency graph (lines 523-529) to avoid import errors
- Use the canonical type signatures (lines 690-736) exactly as specified
- The JSONL format example (lines 261-264) is the authoritative serialization format
- Error handling table (lines 606-610) defines the exception strategy for each error category
- Performance budgets are documented: save() ~100μs, get_last_n() ~1ms per 100 lines, compute_stats() ~5μs per run

No blockers, no ambiguities, no missing specifications. Ready for parallel implementation.
review.summary: APPROVED. Architecture fully satisfies all 11 acceptance criteria with precise, unambiguous interfaces. All PRD requirements are traceable to specific components. Internal consistency validated across all sections. Complexity is appropriate: simple JSONL storage with clean layered architecture justified by testability. No scope creep detected. All risks from PRD are mitigated. Autonomous agents can implement this safely without additional clarification.
